content:
{
  "title": "Dynamic Error-Driven Prompting for Code Generation in LLMs",
  "problem": "Current code generation prompts for large language models often fail to address model-specific error patterns, leading to repetitive mistakes in generated code. There is a need for prompting strategies that dynamically adapt to model weaknesses.",
  "motivation": "Improving LLM-based code generation is vital as these models are increasingly used in real-world software development. Static prompts do not leverage feedback from previous model outputs, missing a chance to correct systematic errors. Adaptive prompting could unlock significant improvements without retraining models.",
  "method": "We propose a novel dynamic prompting approach in which prompts are augmented with synthetic feedback generated by automatic static analysis tools (e.g., pylint/flake8 for Python) applied to early code generations. The prompt is iteratively updated: after an initial code generation, the prompt is prepended with concise error summaries and specific suggestions (e.g., 'Previous code failed due to undefined variable x. Ensure all variables are defined.'). The model is then re-prompted with this augmented context for code regeneration. This process repeats until no critical errors are reported or a maximum number of iterations is reached.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on HumanEval and MBPP datasets, comparing our method against standard zero-shot and few-shot prompting. Metrics include pass@1, pass@10, and static analysis error rates. A small-scale experiment will test if a single iteration of error-driven feedback reduces errors in code generation compared to baseline prompts."
}
